title: Executive Order 13201—Notification of Employee Rights Concerning Payment of Union Dues or Fees

flags.justification: The Secretary of Labor is granted broad administration, enforcement, and exemption authority over the entire federal-contractor 'Beck rights' notice regime.

Genuine accountability mechanisms: agencies must report enforcement results back to the Secretary (Sec.7), and the Secretary must publish the names of noncomplying contractors (Sec.6(c)) - a real public-facing transparency measure.
Not applicable - single coherent subject (union-dues notice requirement).

Sec.3(a) permits the Secretary to exempt a contracting department or agency whenever 'special circumstances require... in order to serve the national interest' - the same undefined self-determined 'national interest' standard this stream has tracked since GHW Bush's EO12800 (this stream, Batch 8).

A genuinely self-interested veto embedded in the enforcement mechanism: a contracting-agency head can block a debarment or blacklisting directive against contracts under their own purview merely by 'continu[ing] personally to object' (Sec.6(a)-(b)) - the agency being investigated effectively holds veto power over its own enforcement.
Not applicable - an ongoing procurement-notice policy, no sunset expected.
No independent-oversight-blocking mechanism.

The order includes an affirmative government-litigation-support commitment (Sec.6(a) proviso) for contractors sued by subcontractors as a result of enforcement direction - a reasonably well-aligned third-party incentive structure.

Revives the identical notice requirement first imposed by GHW Bush's EO12800 (this stream, Batch 8) then revoked by Clinton's EO12836 (this stream: 16.67%) - continuing this stream's tracked cross-administration partisan reversal pattern on federal labor-relations orders.

Sec.3(b)-(c) enumerate an extensive set of exemption categories: overseas work, state-law conflicts, non-union sites, worker-count thresholds, subcontract-tier thresholds, and facility-separateness carve-outs.

notes: Revives the Beck-rights federal-contractor union-dues notice requirement (reversing Clinton's EO12836, this stream: 16.67%), with Secretary of Labor administration, enforcement, and exemption authority. Scores 27.78% (5/18) - notable for a genuinely self-interested enforcement veto (Flag5: the contracting agency being investigated can block its own debarment directive by objecting), an undefined self-determined national-interest exemption standard (Flag4) continuing this stream's tracked EO12800 lineage, and an extensive exemptions architecture (Flag11) - substantially offset by real public-facing noncompliance-list transparency (Flag2 ABSENT).

Bundles three structurally distinct actions: (1) a new 25-member FACA Commission, (2) a new standing departmental 'Initiative' office, and (3) revocation of EO12900 (this stream: 16.67%) - directly paralleling the same bundling pattern identified at EO13218 (this batch, Chunk 1: 43.75%).
Reporting content and deadlines are specifically itemized (Sec.4, Sec.7(a)-(b)), not left to vague discretion.
Not applicable - a commission/office-establishment order, no incentive-gaming structure at issue.

Sec.5: 'any functions of the President under [FACA], except that of reporting to the Congress, shall be performed by the Department of Education' - the standard FACA carve-out.
Not applicable - a commission/office-establishment order, not itself a third-party grant program.
Establishes durable new institutional capacity tied to the 'No Child Left Behind' accountability framework, revoking a Clinton-era predecessor commission (EO12900) covering the same subject.
A single-department (Education) structure with clearly designated ex officio members from three other agencies; no competing claims.
Not applicable - no exemption categories at issue.

notes: Establishes both the 25-member President's Advisory Commission on Educational Excellence for Hispanic Americans and a permanent 'White House Initiative' office within Education, revoking EO12900 (this stream: 16.67%). Scores 31.25% (5/16), driven by concentrated dual-structure authority (Flag1), genuine bundling of commission-creation, office-creation, and revocation actions (Flag3), a genuine sunset (Flag6) that notably applies only to the Commission and not the permanent Initiative office, and the standard FACA carve-out (Flag7) - substantially offset by a genuinely detailed, multi-layered reporting structure (Flag2 and Flag4 both ABSENT).

title: Executive Order 13231—Critical Infrastructure Protection in the Information Age

flags.justification: The Board's Chair, who also serves as 'Special Advisor to the President for Cyberspace Security,' is separately delegated original Top Secret classification authority (Sec.13) - an unusually significant and rare grant of original classification power to an advisory-board chair position, extremely uncommon across this entire corpus.

No explicit Congressional or public reporting requirement appears anywhere in this extremely long and detailed order beyond internal OMB budget coordination (Sec.9(a)) - a notable gap given the order's exceptional scope and the classification-authority delegation.

An extraordinarily extensive bundling of functions within one order: establishing the Critical Infrastructure Protection Board, establishing NIAC, delegating original classification authority, restructuring NSTAC's advisory relationship, revoking EO13130, and creating eleven separate standing committees spanning nearly every domain of the federal government - among the most extensively bundled orders in the entire corpus.

Functions are described in extremely broad, aspirational terms throughout ('coordinate,' 'assist,' 'propose policies') with minimal binding standards; Sec.4(c)'s balancing language ('security should enable, and not unnecessarily impede... business operations') is inherently vague.
Not applicable - a governance-structure order responding to, but not itself invoking, blocking/emergency authority.

NIAC carries an explicit 2-year sunset (Sec.10(e)(ii)) 'unless extended by the President' - though notably the Board ITSELF has no sunset at all, an asymmetry paralleling EO13230's (this chunk) Commission/Initiative asymmetry.

NIAC's FACA carve-out is present (Sec.10(e)(i): 'except that of reporting to the Congress') though it applies only to NIAC, not to the Board itself (composed of federal officials and thus outside FACA's scope).
Not applicable - a governance-coordination order, not itself a third-party grant/contract program (though it coordinates extensively with private-sector infrastructure owners via NIAC).

Established the foundational institutional framework for federal cybersecurity and critical-infrastructure-protection governance in the immediate post-9/11 era, delegating original classification authority and creating an enormous inter-agency coordination apparatus that shaped two decades of subsequent cybersecurity policy, eventually evolving into DHS's CISA.

Despite the order's enormous complexity, Sec.4 is explicit that 'this order does not alter the existing authorities or roles' of OMB, DOD, DCI, or other departments - a deliberate, carefully-drafted anti-cannibalization design choice given the scale of coordination required across dozens of agencies.
Not applicable - a governance-coordination order, no traditional exemption architecture at issue.

notes: Establishes the President's Critical Infrastructure Protection Board (roughly 20 formal members plus eleven standing committees) and the 30-member National Infrastructure Advisory Council, delegating original Top Secret classification authority to the Board's Chair. Scores 50.0% (8/16) - among the most complex orders in the corpus, driven by the rare classification-authority delegation (Flag1), no reporting mechanism (Flag2), extraordinarily extensive functional bundling across eleven committees (Flag3), broad aspirational standards (Flag4), and a CRITICAL Flag9 given its role as the foundational precursor to modern federal cybersecurity governance - substantially offset by explicit, deliberate anti-cannibalization drafting preserving existing agency authorities (Flag10 ABSENT).

title: Executive Order 13233—Further Implementation of the Presidential Records Act

flags.justification: Creates a dual-veto structure in which EITHER the former President OR the incumbent President can indefinitely block release of Presidential records: the Archivist may not permit access 'unless and until the incumbent President advises the Archivist that the former President and the incumbent President agree to authorize access... or until so ordered by a final and nonappealable court order' (Sec.3(d)) - an extraordinarily concentrated blocking power held by two individuals with no time limit for either to decide.
No external reporting or public disclosure requirement documents how often or how many records requests are blocked under this dual-veto process - a closed, opaque procedure.
Not applicable - single coherent subject (Presidential Records Act implementation).

The underlying constitutional privilege standards ('compelling circumstances,' 'demonstrated, specific need') are inherently open-ended judicially-derived doctrines now embedded in a formal blocking procedure without further specification of when they apply.
Not applicable - a records-access procedural order, no incentive-gaming structure at issue.
Not applicable - the statutory 12-year post-presidency window (44 U.S.C. 2204) is set by statute, not this order; the order's own dual-veto mechanism carries no independent time limit.

Reverses two decades of presidential-records transparency policy by shifting the default from Archivist-administered FOIA-style processing (as reinforced by GHW Bush's own EO12667, this stream: 62.5%) to a regime where either President can indefinitely withhold records absent a nonappealable court order - among the most consequential documented reversals of an existing accountability mechanism in this entire corpus.
Not applicable - a records-access order, not a third-party grant/contract program.

A landmark, historically controversial order that became a persistent flashpoint of Congressional and public-transparency criticism throughout the Bush administration, and was ultimately reversed in its entirety by Obama's EO13489 (2009) - a major, contested structural shift in the government's presidential-records transparency architecture.
A single, clear Archivist/former-President/incumbent-President process; no competing agency claims.

Sec.10 allows a former President to designate representatives (including, after death, family members choosing among themselves) to exercise these indefinite blocking rights on his behalf - a notable, unusual 'inheritable' privilege-holder category; Sec.11 separately carves out special treatment for Vice Presidential records.

notes: Reverses the presumption favoring eventual disclosure established by GHW Bush's EO12667 (this stream: 62.5%), creating a dual-veto structure in which either the former or incumbent President can indefinitely block release of Presidential records absent a nonappealable court order. Scores 64.29% (9/14) - one of the highest-scoring orders in the corpus, with THREE CRITICAL findings: concentrated dual-veto blocking power (Flag1), reversal of an existing accountability mechanism (Flag7), and lasting historical/political controversy culminating in Obama's full 2009 reversal via EO13489 (Flag9). A landmark diff-aware WEAKENING finding, standing in sharp contrast to this stream's more common diff-aware improvement pattern - notable as one of the most consequential accountability rollbacks documented in the entire corpus.
